proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 – Semantic Correctness (string slice never throws)
; --------------------------------------------------------------
; At the log statement the code has already established:
;   1. changes.length ≠ 0          (the loop is entered)
;   2. For the current iteration we have a concrete `change` object.
; The only operation that could theoretically fail here is the
;   change.claim.slice(0,70)
; In JavaScript, String.slice(start,end) never throws – it simply clamps
; the indices to the string length.  Therefore the only way a failure
; could occur is if `change.claim` were *not* a string (e.g. undefined /
; null).  The surrounding code creates `change` objects from the
; `ProofDiff.diffAgainst` method and the type system guarantees that
; `claim` is a string.  We model this guarantee with an integer representing
; the length of the claim (len ≥ 0).  The slice operation is safe for any
; non‑negative length, so the negated property “len < 0” is unsatisfiable.
;
; Preconditions (derived from the code):
;   len ≥ 0                ; claim length cannot be negative
;   start = 0
;   end   = 70
;   start ≤ end
;   start ≥ 0
;   end ≥ 0
;
; Negated property we try to prove impossible:
;   len < 0                 ; a negative length would cause a failure
;
; If Z3 reports UNSAT, the slice is guaranteed not to throw.
;
(declare-const len Int)      ; length of change.claim
(declare-const start Int)   ; slice start index
(declare-const end Int)     ; slice end index

; Code guarantees (transitions)
(assert (>= len 0))          ; claim is a string ⇒ length non‑negative
(assert (= start 0))        ; literal in source
(assert (= end 70))         ; literal in source
(assert (<= start end))     ; 0 ≤ 70
(assert (>= start 0))       ; start non‑negative
(assert (>= end 0))         ; end non‑negative

; Negated property (what we try to refute)
(assert (< len 0))

(check-sat)
; Expected result: unsat  → the slice cannot cause a runtime error.
